body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: 2. Applying BGP-LS SRv6 Extensions to BGP-LS-SPF

transcript.text: Remember to sign in to the um meet echo. Yeah, let's go ahead and start it. You ready to go? >> Okay. Uh let's get started. Um welcome to the RSV working group session on ITF123. And as always, this session is being recorded. Okay, here's a note. Well, uh this is the remainder of IT policies on patents and the code of conduct. uh since it's already Thursday, I assume most of you have already noted very well and if not please read this carefully and here's a note really well this again it's about the ITF guide guidelines for conduct and this is important for collaboration in ITF and for the uh proper behavior to each Other here are the meeting tips for the IT meeting for both the inerson participants and also the remote participants. Basically we would need to use maco both for the blue sheet and also for the discussion on the mic. And here are the resources for the meeting. And here is the agenda for this session. We have the working group updates and then we will continue the discussion about the BGPSF neighbor disco discovery. We have two proposed solutions and we will continue the discussion on this session and the last one is uh one individual draft on the extension or applicability of SR6 to BGP SPF. Here are the working group updates uh since last meeting. We have two new RFC's uh the base SPF document and also its applicability in the data center both has been uh published as RFC. So congratulations to the authors and also the shepherds on their hard work. And for the other uh working group drafts, the BGP RS young document has expired since uh April 24, 2024. And according to the feedback from the authors, they are working on the resolution of the received comments and they have planned to provide an update after this meeting. Uh for the L3DL draft, it's on today's agenda. uh there's a recent update uh to address some of the security directory review comments and we still need to continue this discussion and then respond to the liaison from ITE 802.1 um after last ITF uh we had a working group inter meeting on in on May six uh the meeting materials and notes can be found on this page. on this data tracker. We had presentations on both L3DL and LLDP based neighbor discovery mechanisms. We also had good discussion on the similarity and difference between the two proposals and whether all the requirements for peer discovery can be met by these solutions. There were some questions and discussion continued on the mailing list. However, we haven't reached the conclusion on how to proceed and respond to this leazison yet. So, we need to continue the discussion during this session. We also invited the colleagues from IV21 to join us join the session. Thank you very much. uh regarding the requirements on the BGPS SPF pure discovery uh there's no standalone requirement document on this but we have some text about the requirements on this uh connection discovery and livveness detection both in RFC uh 9815 and 9816 in 98 uh 15 it says that for the peering models uh described in the section 4.2 and 4.3 direct connection discovery and livveness detection for the interconnecting links are independent of the BGB protocol. So if this indicates uh there's some other mechanism needed for this then in the FC 9816 uh it provides a peer discovery considerations and requirements in the data center scenario in the section 5 5.4. Uh basically the basic functionality of peer discovery is to discover the address of the single hop pier and it also mentions that it may be useful to have the following information related to the BGP session being discovered including uh the autonomous system number the BGP ID and also security capability the session policy identifier which can be used to associate with uh common session parame parameters with a peer and also it can be used to install the roads towards the potential peer loop back address. Uh the last one is about there is a requirements to establish a multihop session with the controllers in some of the period models described there. So I think this can be some information uh of basis for the today's discussion may be helpful. Okay. Uh any question or comments on this slides or the information provided here or >> Okay. >> Okay. >> So let's move on to the first uh topic of today's session. Um I think for the discussion maybe we can first let the L3D authors to give some brief update of that draft recently. So maybe uh Q is busy. Yeah. There's the agenda here. >> Oh, you are you are going to do okay. Do you want to come? >> Uh yeah. uh K Patel Arcus um we haven't made a significant progress from the last ITF but um I believe the um idea of using and piggybacking on LLDP v1 uh for the hellos is something that we are considering um and hopefully in next few weeks we will have uh um our side of the already sorted out pretty well. Yeah. So that's a short update from my our side as an authors. Okay. Thank you. Uh is there any comment on this uh L3D draft? Uh we or we will move on to the presentation of the LDP1. Okay. So Paul, >> uh, hi. >> I will share the slides for you and you can have the control. >> All right. So, uh, >> let me wait. >> So, how do I control the slides? Let me try to stop. >> G's bringing him up. Then he can transfer control. And you should see something on the left. I mean on the right. >> Yeah, >> on the right says you got slide control. >> Okay. All right. I hear a lot of echo. Is it coming through okay out there? >> Yeah, you're coming through loud and clear. I hope everybody has the onsite tool on here and nobody's using the full client because that could cause an echo. >> All right. Yeah, still not not too bad here anyway. All right. So I I have um just a review of some of the things that we've been at. So just as a recap, you know, we do have a leaison from the ITLE E 802.1 working group uh regarding the use of LDP uh for the discovery and also uh we have extended LDP to allow for large data sets which might be required uh for LSVR. We also have a number of contributions in front of the committee uh which um include materials um draft BTORF which was just updated by the way to um version 01. So LDP uh is basically a database exchange protocol and the way that works is that information like for instance the addresses that are discovered are stored in TLVs within the database uh which can be represented in Yang models and LDP is then responsible for advertising that database information across to all the neighbor nodes. So, it's a very simple database exchange protocol. So, >> there's one question. There's somebody at the queue. We'll let them uh answer question. It's uh Anoir. >> Yes. >> Right. Did you mean to be at the queue? >> Okay, I'm removed. >> Please continue. >> Yeah, please continue. >> All right. All right. So, so this >> was one of those butt cues. So this is just a you know a quick summary but basically there is LDP uh version one and LDP version two. The version one LDP uses a single frame which was the maximum size of the database which has been extended by it e now to allow a large number of frames uh for the database. So you can have large sizes with LVP DP version 2 or XLDP as it's referred to at times. Uh so I had wanted to go into a little more detail about LDP in terms of reliability. Uh so LDP basically uses a multiccast hello of a single packet which is based on LDP version one. Uh that single packet is transmitted periodically but also on any change event within the database is transmitted immediately. Not only is it transmitted immediately, but it's also repeated on a fast timer for a period of time to make sure that it picks up right away. The extension frames uh which are those beyond the single uh initial hello frame are identified by a TLV uh inside the database that is carried in the initial frame and that informs the receiver of all the potential uh PDUs that need to be transferred. Those PDUs are transferred on uniccast addresses and are uh based on requests from the receiver which uh basically can retry those requests in the event information is lost. So basically you have uh a uniccast transmission of frames and the frames that are transmitted are based on only those frames that need to be updated. So um the manifest TLV as it's called which includes the information for all the extended PDUs contains a digest and information that is used to determine whether there's any change in each one of the PDUs. And so if there's no change in the PDU then the receiver doesn't request any updating. Okay. uh there are other um I had one to point out there's one other uh advantage to this and that is that this is driven by the receiver uh and so that does have an advantage from the point of view of processing load. The receiver knows how fast it can process the incoming frames and can basically pace the information at a rate that is acceptable to its processing rate. And this this is important when you have control planes that may be bogged down doing other things uh or with limited buffer spaces which is not uncommon uh you know inside uh routers. So I had want to talk some about addressing. Um draft Burf uh describes using a nearest router address for uh basically the LDP multiccast which is equivalent to the hello packet. Uh that's because in this example which is an example of switch virtual interfaces. This is a common router arrangement where the switch virtual interface is actually generated by a bridging layer that sits on the physical network and separates the routing layer from the physical layer and is used then to direct information to um the router switch virtual interfaces. By using a nearest router uh multiccast address, the address can transfer through these bridges that sit at the base of the routers and generate switch virtual circuits all the way into um the actual router physical port. Okay. So um so my recommendation has been to use uh a nearest router scope MAC uh for LDP which will then be something that you know would be selected by Ayanna for instance and specified specifically for this application. So uh the L3DL protocol uh identifies each one of the router ports which with a logical link endpoint identifier and in effect LDP also has the equivalent identifier which is the chassis and port ID. Um if what's done is you use the nearest router uh multiccast address then uh the chassis and port ID are fully equivalent to the identifier of the LLI in L3DL and so uh they represent then the actual address of the router port within the end device and so can be used there. Um, the LDP uh can have an ITF, organizationally specific TLB set which can carry all the addressing information and other information that you wish uh about the uh uh edge ports. um these would have to be defined and draft Burf identifies how to define all the TLVs that are equivalent to L3DL. Right? So all the information that L3DL transfers is included in draft BTORF TLBs and they're encoded in this uh format where you actually have um an organizationally specific LDPT which is identified by an IETF uh OUI and a subtype which would identify LSVR and then an LSVR type that would identify the TLV identifier which would then identify what the encapsulation types were etc. So from the point of view of liveless uh LDP itself is a liveless protocol. Uh it sends uh and specifies a time to live and repeats based on a timer. The granularity of time to live though for LDP is in seconds might be many seconds actually. Um it e also supports though um connectivity fault management which is an additional protocol that is a a endto-end management protocol for L2 networks and um it basically can give you liveless down into the millisecond range. It's minimum time granularity is about 10 milliseconds. Uh finally um the Ethernet committee 802.13 also supports an OEM livveness which only operates on a link bylink basis but nevertheless can give granularity down into the microscond range for very fast recovery. And this can then be propagated up to the router to indicate you know a a failure uh occurring very quickly. So uh I have my summary for uh applying LDP to the link state virtual routing discovery and configuration. Um so we've seen that you know discovery can be achieved by running LDP at the link state virtual router uh logical link endpoints right um which is enabled by using this nearest router group address that would have to be assigned um and that are identified by the LDP chassis ID and port ID which identifies the database endpoints. Um you know we have discovery uh of L3 and L2 and a half encapsulations using the TLVs which are documented in draft Burrf. Um and so the database would be built with all the TLDS which you know can be a large number of addresses very large number of addresses. [Music] So on top of LDP though LDP would only build the database with the information. there would need to be an application sitting on top of LDP that would extract the database information and validate it. So that that's something that would have to be done um by the the committee in addition to using LDP. So LDP wouldn't be the final step. It's it's providing all the information that's needed, but that information would have to be reduced by an application inside the router, right? Um so any of the interfaces um can be used. So I've only uh shown an example of a switch virtual interface but the same techniques will work for all for direct physical interfaces or for sub interfaces or any other type of uh router interface arrangement. Uh which is one of the beauties of the scheme. You have one scheme which works in the most complicated case but also works in all the simple cases also. Um so L3 livessless um can be enabled simply by a uh indication in the TLVs which is also what's done in L3DL um and L2 liveless uh there are options uh if you just use LDP uh then you get liveless in the order of second ranges but you could use connectivity default management also which would give you millisecond ranges of liveless. So uh so the so you can also communicate all the parameters needed through the TLVs for BGP. um NLDP can also be secured with max which is the ITLE E technique for um security. So I guess uh as it stands I'm requesting the adoption of draft BTORF uh for LSVR discovery. And so that's the end of my presentation. Thank you, Paul. And AC, you're in the queue. >> Yeah. AC Linda Marcus, you said something interesting that the committee would have to do about uh are you seeing standardizing the application interface to disseminate the information to other uh >> well as part of something that would have to be standardized. I I I I didn't really understand that statement. >> Right. So it might not have to be standardized but nevertheless um LDP doesn't talk directly to BGP. It builds a database of the information and then um the LSV router would have to you know uh validate and reduce that information. >> Right. As you'd have to do with any discovery mechanism. >> Exactly. And so but LDP doesn't do that for you. No, I think implementers understand that. >> Yes. Okay. and Russ. So my understanding is that the um authors of L3P are looking or are looking at this technique for replacing the hello message uh that is in the current draft. So that they um LLDP will be used um for the all of the layer 2 uh discovery pieces and then um the everything else will happen at layer three um including the um livveness check which will be done with BFD. That's I believe the direction we're going. Is that not right? So BFD is always an option which can be enabled in LDP also. Okay. Yeah, K Patel Arcus, uh, we have a reasons to believe that you don't want to pollute BFD for this. Um, we want to keep it quite simple to exchange the endpoint information. So, um, I think Russ, you summarized it perfectly. Thank you. >> Okay. Uh, next is myself. Uh as a working group member, I'd like to have a quick clarification question on this uh TLV uh 1277. and you'll go that go to that page >> uh TLV >> um LLDPF organizationally specific TLV. >> Oh, right. So, so right. So, um >> yeah, >> the organizationally specific TLB. Right. Uh so so this is this is part of the LDP standard. >> Yeah. >> Which basically provides a way for organizations to specify uh TLBs of their own. Okay. And it specifies this format which is um all LDPTLBs are type length encoded um with a seven bit type and a 9 bit length and the um organizationally specific TLVs are then identified immediately by an OUI which identifies the standardizing body. Now this OUI is an IETF OUI which is owned by Ayanna and can be used for this purpose right and so past that then there's a subtype so up to this level this is the organization that is required for LDP through the subtype then pass. The subtype is all information that is uh up to the committee uh the organizer whoever owns this OUI to define. >> Okay. So it means that all these uh ITF uh specific information will be carried under this TLV right for >> that's right and it'll be compatible with all LDP implementations >> and is it allowed to carry multiple this uh TLV in the >> Yes absolutely and so actually draft bour specifies that but uh um the TLBs are limited in size um to 512 bytes and 511 bytes actually but uh um you can have as many of them as you like in the database. So if you have a lot of addresses you can have a hundred of these TLBs in there. Each TLB can carry a bunch of addresses. >> Okay. Thank you. You see, are you still in the queue? >> No. >> Okay, Kaitton, please. Yeah. Hi, Paul. Uh, Paul, thanks for the presentation. Uh, I saw that uh you had asked for adoption. Uh I also heard that uh Russ and Cur mentioned that uh uh the L3DL uh draft authors are looking at leveraging LLDP for the discovery part I understand and then build the other TLVs on top of it into L3DL. That's I think what I understood. Uh I would like to know your views on that. >> Right. So so first of all uh it is viable to do that. Okay. Technically that can work. Uh I guess the the other view that I would have is that I'm not sure it's necessary to have another protocol since offhand uh you know I don't see the real advantage to L3DL in terms of uh the transfers you can transfer as much information uh I believe with um extended LDP and I believe the reliability is you know equivalent or better and the industry support is broader. Um okay uh thanks for that. If I may have a follow- on question. Uh you referred to uh the draft ACR LLDPR discovery uh which is again sharing more information for a different use case than LSPR. >> Yes. Uh and uh uh could you clarify the relationship between or how you see the relationship between uh uh draft uh you know your draft and this draft? >> Right. The the reason I mentioned this draft is that this draft is using this format of uh >> the subtyping, right? Uh which then is duplicated here and within draft autof which basically uses a subtype that identifies the the committee. So this would be LSVR in this case and then another subtype to identify the specific type of TLV. That's the part that is actually pulled from um uh draft a >> I see. Um okay. Uh I mean of of course it's for the working group to decide. uh but uh as far as I know uh we in ITF under ITF Aana there is actually perhaps maybe a I mean it's not precluded to have a flat uh code point space under uh ITF Ayana which means that uh another level of hierarchy may or may not be necessary in that case but >> anyway >> that's a technical part okay >> right and so I would agree with that point of view. So >> you would need a registry. The regist this this the the the draft AC BGP neighbor discovery was the was the first one to allocate subtypes. So it you would need an insert. >> Right. >> Sure. Okay. Thank you. Uh and question to the chairs. uh what's your uh plan on handling the adoption request and reconciling with the other thing that we heard? Do you have how do we plan to do that? Uh I mean maybe not not discuss here but uh but think about >> Yeah. Ko please. Kelus. I think we should take this discussion on the mailing list because we really want to have a a one more time a detailed conversation as to what were the motiv motivations um with uh L3DL versus um LLDP based mechanism. I again want to be super clear that the authors are looking at uh portions of LLDP um to see how we can leverage and build upon it um such that you can sort of come across and leverage LLDP as much as possible. But there are reasons why we came up with L3DL and those reasons should be well discussed on the mailing list as opposed to here. >> Okay. Thank you. And I I just want to add one more thing that I'm an author on both the drafts. So um I can tell you pros and cons of why one versus another and as a routing vendor um what makes sense, what doesn't make sense from a vendor perspective. So I think that would be a good conversation to have on the mailing list. >> Yeah, that makes sense to me. I'm just I'm just capturing this in the notes, the minutes. >> Any other comments? >> Um yeah, so I'm all for debating this on the mailing list. Um after the interim, we had such a suggestion and there wasn't actually much back and forth as a result of that. Okay. Then uh yeah, I would really encourage the discussion to actually happen on the mailing list between this and the next ITF. uh if I mean yeah that's the best way to engage as Kor mentioned on this and uh come to a resolution all the discussion analysis happen there thank you >> thank you H thank you Ken >> the chairs would like to see a decision as well be on the before the next IETF >> do we need another interim if necessary. >> So please use the meeting list for the discussion. Yeah. >> Okay. >> And thank you Paul. >> Thank you. And now we come to the second presentation. Uh, it's from Djang. >> Yeah. Can you hear me? >> Yeah. Do you want me to hand you the stats control? >> Uh, okay. I I can't control. Okay. >> Okay. >> Okay. Hello everyone. This is NA from Huawei. I will present the draft on applying BGP RS segment routing over IPA6 extensions to BDP SPF on behalf of my colleagues. Okay. And uh so in this version we enrich the existing sections with the TR usage and also the formats. And we also added the advertising and point behaviors section to describe the appointment behaviors that can be advertised by BPSF. And uh we also added the acknowledgement section to thank those those who uh who provide valuable comments for this draft. Okay. Okay. Let's start from the SR6 node attributes Tways. The first one is SR6 T. It is used to announce the SY6 related capabilities of the node is a monpf node attribute and must be advertised only once. If uh if the SR6 cop is not advertised then the node then it indicates the node does not support SR 6. And the second Tway is SR6 node MSD type Tway. It is used to advertise the limits and segment routine harder operation supported by the SR6 cable node. It is optional TRV and must be other test only once. If there are multiple uh MSD types that have the same code point in node MSD T then the node MS must be ignored by the receiver. And uh the third one is the S agra sim. It is used to advertise as six agims supported by the node. It is also an optional TR and uh AR zero is supported by default. They must be advertised only once. Uh if SR6 cable node does not advertise this T, it implies the algorithm zero is the only agm supported by the node. And uh if originating node adward has the as uh a sim kway then the agim zero must be present. Okay from from this page I think the following several pages where we introduced the s6 link attributes t. The first one is SRC uh six and and X theory that is used to advertise as six says corresponding to a point to a point or J of the node. It is also an optional TRA and uh more than one instance of this TR can be included in the BP SPF attribute. uh uh multiple SY6 and X C may be associated with the same agency and uh all the and X must be a subnet of locator and uh the next one is uh layer to bundle memory it is used to advertise the layer 2 bundle member link attributes when the layer 2 bundle member link is associated with a part uh layer three link considering that BP SPF may also includes the trunk link scenarios. So BPSf may also support it. uh it is an optional TR and uh multiple there to bundle member attribute TRS may be associated with the link right and uh the advertisement must be with if the link becomes operationally down or it is no longer a member of the uh bundle and uh the third one is the SR6 link MS SD type it is used to otherwise the deis and SI operation supported on the specific link uh by the SR V6 cable node. It is also an optional T and must be advertised only once. Okay. And uh the SR6 prefix attributes TR uh only includes one T which is S6 locator T. They is used to advertise locator supported by the node. Multiple SR6 locator T may be advertised with with a different locator. If multiple TAS are received for the same locator then the receiver must use the first occurrence and uh the since the flag fails are not used in BPSf so it should be set to zero and uh also because BPSF defines the prefix metric T is monitory for prefix NR so the metric failed in this T is no longer useful. So it also should be set to zero. Okay. PPRs also define the SR to carry the SY 6 information. The S set includes the Sway 6 S information. It is used to advertise the SY 6 assocated with a node in S6 S. is a monetary TRA in the SR6 NRI and each NR must contain a single SR6 information and uh uh the SR6 seed must be allocated from it associated locator. This is uh the same as the the same as the previous I think is uh uh and x set. Okay. And uh there are also some uh attributes for the s. All right. The first is SR6 and the point of behavior TR. It is used to advertise the behaviors associated with SID. It's a monetary T and must be included once and uh the SY6 structure T is used to advertise the length of each individual part of the SY6 seed is an optional T and uh uh must not appear more than once. It uh it can be used uh for the and access state and uh and the uh and say and this theory must uh the sum of the different uh paths of this theory must be less than or equal to 122 bits 28 bits. Okay, we also added a new section which is the advertising and important behaviors to to uh to introduce which behaviors can are supported in BP SPF and uh okay so for the next steps we welcome for comments and also suggestions and uh we also So think we have made a bigger progress since the last version. Uh is it ready for working group to consider adop okay that's all thank you I guess I'm the only one in the queue. Uh the one thing I I'd like to see I see you the the TLVs are much better defined in the validation you know of these existing uh BGPLS um advertisements but what I'd like to see I'm not going to make you do pseudo code like is in the base document 9815 but it would like to just you know it's a pretty simple single it's equivalent to an IGP single area SPF kind just go at a high level talk about when you use these and what you install for each one with and you can use references to the SR architecture you don't have to you know reinvent that but it would be rather than just have a definition of TLVs do what the IGP documents describe when you're, you know, when you're using these TLBs and what you're installing in the um both the the in in the in the ribbon with or in the um the I guess I guess everything for SRV6 is in the IPv6 uniccast RI. or I guess you have you even have algorithm specific uh TLVs. So does that imply that we're doing flex algorithm? I mean I mean I I think you need if you're going to have all this functionality, you need more definition than just the TLVs. You can't just say, "Well, the IGPS do this. Here's the TLV." At least you at least need to describe it at a high level and you could reference the applicable uh sections in in the OPF or ISIS documents, you know, at least for each of these behaviors because you've got probably with the TLBs, the background on this is probably tens if not hundreds of pages of uh LSR RFC's. >> Yes. Okay. Uh so you we can we can remove the the red definitions in this draft. Uh which >> I mean I I mean I I'm not I'm not telling you you have to do pseudo code like is in the base document. You know, we did that for the first one, but that was a choice. But you really need to point >> to how you're using the LSP the these TLVs, these SRV6 TLVs, and what you're what you're installing. >> Okay. So, so I I don't understand what you me installing >> what you're installing in uh in in the IPv6 rib and which rib which rib the TLV's uh you know what yeah where where you're installing it what you're installing what what where you're installing these SIDs. Uh okay. Maybe we can we can continue to discuss this in the main list. >> Sure. >> Okay. >> And maybe you can check the content in the IGPSR6 documents and see whether something could be referenced, right? >> Yes. Yes. >> I mean it would I think it would be good. I mean it's a simple it's a it's it's a simple single area SPF and you could go in as you're processing you know say as you're processing the nodes uh you examine all the you know these these BGPL BGP SPF or BGLS SPF uh TLVs and what you install I mean I don't think it's that hard but just having the TLVs and detailed explanation of the validation of the TLVs without saying how you're going to use them or or even having a reference. I don't think that's enough. >> Okay. Okay. Thank you. Any other comments? Oh, I'm I'm off too. >> Okay. Uh, thank you, Lee. >> Thank you. And we have uh five minutes remaining. Maybe Ken want to say something. Uh just a quick reminder that uh following the IETF meeting there is an ITLE E plenary 802.1 happening in the same location and uh you know there were few participants from the ITE E side probably here I see Glenn uh okay yeah thanks for waving your hand so please do use the opportunity to discuss uh in the hallways uh today tomorrow or next week if you are staying on and uh look to see us converge on a solution soon. >> So is our ad going to stay on? >> Yes, until Saturday. >> Oh, until this Saturday, day after tomorrow, but don't expect me to solve this problem. Okay, I guess I guess we're done. I'm not We're going to kind of watch the drafts and everything to determine if we're going to meet in um uh where is it next? Montreal. >> So, see you in Montreal. >> Yeah. >> Thank you. as to at least the career. >> The mailing list. >> Yeah. >> You mean what? >> Yeah. >> Please use it. >> Yeah. Please use the mailing list. We'll see you on the mailing list. >> That's what they said. >> Yeah. Let's uh continue the discussion on the list. So, where do you live? What? >> Friday.

uh inside the database that is carried

only those frames that need to be

contains a digest and information that
